fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need a pluggable logging thing built out — basically a central hub where you can plug in different log listeners and they all get the same log call forwarded to them. Think of it like the broadcast pattern we used in the notification module a while back, but for logs. The main pain points we keep hearing from devs are: (1) every call site has its own boilerplate for deciding what to log and how to format it, and (2) when we want to swap out logging behavior for tests or crash reporting, we end up touching dozens of files. So we want one static entry point that everyone uses, and the destinations handle their own filtering logic. There's also been complaints about log spam in production — destinations should be able to say 'only give me warnings and above' or 'only give me logs from this specific component.' We also need it to handle attaching exception/error info to messages gracefully. One edge case that bit us before: if someone sets a tag and then the log gets filtered out, that tag should NOT bleed over onto the next log line — same cleanup behavior we had to patch in the auth flow. Long messages that exceed the platform write limit should be split up automatically too. The registry itself needs to reject bad inputs cleanly rather than silently corrupting state.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: LogHub.plant(LogDestination tree) / LogHub.log(Severity priority, String tag, String message, Object... args) / LogHub.uproot(LogDestination tree) / LogHub.uprootAll()

trigger_keywords: severity numeric codes
what number does each log level map to
priority integer values for verbose debug info warn error assert
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The six severities map to fixed numeric codes: verbose=2, debug=3, info=4, warn=5, error=6, assert=7. These codes are stable and must never change — destinations observe the numeric code alongside tag and message.

trigger_keywords: tag consumed when gated out
one-time tag bleeds onto next call
explicit tag not cleared after filtered log
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An explicit one-time tag MUST be consumed by the very next logging call even if that call is skipped by severity or tag gating. The pending tag must never leak onto a subsequent call. After a gated-out call that had an explicit tag set, the next delivered call must carry an empty tag (unless it sets its own explicit tag).

trigger_keywords: null message with no error
empty log call dropped
logging call with no message and no throwable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A logging call whose message is null/absent AND which carries no accompanying error object must be silently swallowed — no record is produced and nothing is delivered to any destination.

trigger_keywords: format args only when supplied
percent sign preserved verbatim
printf interpolation empty args
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Printf-style interpolation is applied ONLY when format arguments are actually supplied. If no arguments are provided (or the argument list is absent/empty), the message is delivered verbatim and any literal percent sequences (e.g., te%st) are preserved untouched without interpolation.

trigger_keywords: registry validation error categories
what errors does plant throw
invalid destination install rejection codes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Registry mutations reject invalid inputs with these stable categories: plant_into_self (installing the hub into itself), null_tree (null single destination), null_trees (null batch), null_tree_in_array (batch containing a null element), uproot_not_planted (removing a destination not in registry). Each rejection emits error=<category> and leaves the registry unchanged.

trigger_keywords: message chunking split limit
long message write threshold
how are oversized log lines broken up
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Messages are first split on newline boundaries. Each resulting line longer than the maximum write length (4000 characters) is further broken into successive pieces of at most 4000 characters each. The chunk length limit is 4000; a 6000-char run produces two chunks of 4000 and 2000.

trigger_keywords: tag derivation rules
automatic tag from class name
strip package name anonymous class markers truncation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Automatic tag derivation: strip the package portion (keep only the simple class name), remove trailing anonymous-class markers (dollar-sign followed by digits at the end), then on platform SDK levels below 26 truncate to a maximum of 23 characters. At SDK 26 or above no truncation occurs. An override mode can append the frame's line number as :lineNumber to the derived tag.

trigger_keywords: error object serialized into message
throwable appended to message text
null message with exception only
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a call supplies both a message and an error object, the error's rendered representation is appended below the message text, starting with the error's own label. When only an error object is supplied (no message, or explicit null message), the error's rendered representation becomes the entire message. The head line of the delivered text is the error's label.

trigger_keywords: batch install destinations
plant_batch multiple at once
install several destinations in one call
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Destinations can be installed individually (one at a time) or as a batch (several at once in a single call). Both methods result in the same installation-order registry. Each destination receives a stable zero-based index assigned in creation order regardless of whether it was installed individually or as part of a batch.

trigger_keywords: custom formatter prefix
destination overrides formatting
format template wrapped by destination
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A destination may supply its own formatting step that wraps the standard printf-style interpolation. For example, a destination configured with a format_prefix prepends that prefix to the format template before arguments are applied. The customized formatting is reflected in the final delivered message text observed by that destination.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'registry', 'plant_error', 'dispatch', 'throwable', 'tag_derive', 'chunk'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the broadcast pattern we used in the notification module a while back
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The LogHub registry's fan-out behavior: every log call is delivered to all currently installed LogDestination instances; see Feature 1.3 broadcast/uproot semantics in start.md and feature1_3_broadcast_uproot.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: same cleanup behavior we had to patch in the auth flow
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The one-time explicit tag consumption rule: a pending tag is always consumed by the next log call even if that call is gated out by severity filtering, preventing tag leakage; see feature4_3_tag_consumed.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Platform SDK level threshold for tag truncation in automatic tag derivation: below SDK 26 tags are truncated to 23 characters, at or above SDK 26 no truncation is applied; see feature6_tag_derivation.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can confirm off the top of my head — could you dig into the existing codebase or test cases to verify the exact behavior? The test files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ should have concrete examples that clarify the expected output.

feature1_1_tree_count.json.description: A central logging hub keeps a live registry of currently installed log destinations. Querying the registry size reflects exactly how many destinations are installed at that moment. Installing additional destinations increases the count by one each, and clearing the registry removes every destination at once, returning the count to zero. The input is a sequence of registry operations (install one destination, query the current count, clear all); the output is one count line per query, in order.

feature1_2_planted_listing.json.description: The registry can be enumerated to obtain a snapshot of every installed destination, in the exact order the destinations were installed. Destinations may be installed one at a time or several at once in a single batch call; either way the snapshot lists all of them in installation order. Each installed destination is identified by a stable zero-based index assigned in the order it was created. The output is a single line listing those indices in registry order.

feature1_3_broadcast_uproot.json.description: A logging call made through the central hub is broadcast to every destination currently installed in the registry; each installed destination receives its own copy. A destination can be removed individually, after which it no longer receives broadcasts while the remaining destinations still do, or the whole registry can be cleared so that subsequent broadcasts reach no one. The input installs some destinations and interleaves broadcasts with removals; the output reports, for each broadcast, how many destinations received it.

feature2_registration_validation.json.description: Registry-mutating operations validate their arguments and reject invalid requests with a stable, language-neutral error category rather than performing the operation. Installing the hub's own aggregate destination into itself is refused as a self-installation. Installing a null destination, installing a null batch, or installing a batch that contains a null element are each refused as the corresponding null-argument category. Removing a destination that was never installed is refused as a not-installed removal. The input names which invalid operation to attempt; the output is a single normalized error line.

feature3_1_priority_levels.json.description: Each logging call carries a severity. The six standard severities form an ordered ladder, and each maps to a fixed numeric severity code that a destination observes alongside the tag and the final message text. A message can be logged either through a severity-specific entry point or through a generic entry point that takes the severity explicitly; both deliver a record carrying the same numeric code. The input is a sequence of logging calls; the output is one delivered record per call, reporting its numeric severity code, its tag, and its message.

cases.expected_output:
priority=2 tag=T message=Hello, World!
priority=3 tag=T message=Hello, World!
priority=4 tag=T message=Hello, World!
priority=5 tag=T message=Hello, World!
priority=6 tag=T message=Hello, World!
priority=7 tag=T message=Hello, World!

feature3_2_argument_formatting.json.description: When a logging call supplies format arguments, the message string is treated as a printf-style format template and is interpolated with those arguments before delivery. When a logging call supplies no arguments at all, the message is delivered verbatim and is NOT interpolated, so a literal percent sequence in the text is preserved untouched. Passing an explicitly empty/absent argument list is equivalent to supplying no arguments. The input is a single logging call with or without arguments; the output is the delivered record showing the final message text.

feature3_3_null_message.json.description: A logging call whose message is absent (null) and which carries no accompanying error object produces no record at all: the call is silently swallowed and nothing is delivered to any destination. The input is a single logging call with no message and no error; the output is empty.

feature3_4_explicit_tag.json.description: A caller may set an explicit one-time tag immediately before a logging call. When set, that explicit tag overrides any automatic tagging and is observed on the delivered record. The input is a logging call preceded by an explicit tag; the output is the delivered record carrying that exact tag.

feature3_5_custom_formatter.json.description: A destination may customize how messages are formatted by supplying its own formatting step that wraps the standard printf-style interpolation, for example by prepending a fixed prefix to the format template before the arguments are applied. The customized formatting is reflected in the final delivered message text. The input configures a destination with a custom formatter prefix and logs one message with an argument; the output is the delivered record showing the prefixed, interpolated text.

cases.expected_output: priority=3 tag=T message=Test formatting: Test message logged. 100

feature4_1_priority_gate.json.description: A destination can gate logging by severity: it declares which severities are loggable, and the hub skips any call whose severity is not loggable so that no record is delivered for it. A destination that is loggable for exactly one severity therefore receives only calls at that severity, regardless of the order calls arrive in. The input configures a destination to accept a single severity and then logs at every severity in turn; the output is only the record for the accepted severity.

feature4_2_tag_gate.json.description: A destination can gate logging by tag: its loggable decision may inspect both the tag and the severity, so it can accept only calls bearing a particular tag and skip all others. A call that bears the accepted tag is delivered; calls without that tag (whatever their severity) are skipped. The input configures a destination that accepts only one tag, logs one call with that tag and several calls without it; the output is only the record for the accepted tag.

feature4_3_tag_consumed.json.description: An explicit one-time tag is consumed by the very next logging call even if that call is skipped by severity gating, so the pending tag never leaks onto a later call. With a destination that gates out low-severity calls, setting an explicit tag and then making a low-severity call (which is skipped) must clear that tag; a subsequent higher-severity call with no explicit tag is then delivered WITHOUT the earlier tag. The input sets a tag, makes a gated-out call, then makes an accepted call with no tag; the output is the accepted record carrying an empty tag.

feature5_1_message_and_throwable.json.description: When a logging call supplies both a message and an accompanying error object, the destination receives a record at the call's severity whose text begins with the supplied message and then has the error object's rendered representation appended below it. The appended rendering starts with the error's own label. The delivered record reports the numeric severity, the tag, the leading (head) line of the text, and a flag confirming the error's rendering was serialized into the message. The behavior is identical whether the call is made on the main flow or from a separately spawned worker. The input is a logging call carrying a message and an error labeled by the case; the output reports the head line and that serialization occurred.

feature5_2_throwable_only.json.description: When a logging call supplies an error object but no message, the error's rendered representation becomes the message itself: the delivered record's text is exactly the serialized rendering, whose leading line is the error's label. This works at every severity, and an explicit tag, if set before each call, is observed on the record. The input logs an error object alone across all severities (optionally with an explicit tag); the output reports each record's numeric severity, tag, the head line (the error label), and that serialization occurred.

cases.expected_output:
priority=2 tag= head=err-a serialized=true
priority=4 tag= head=err-b serialized=true
priority=3 tag= head=err-c serialized=true
priority=5 tag= head=err-d serialized=true
priority=6 tag= head=err-e serialized=true
priority=7 tag= head=err-f serialized=true

feature5_3_null_message_throwable.json.description: When a logging call passes both an explicit null message AND an error object (the message-accepting overload, with no message text), the behavior matches supplying the error alone: the error's rendered representation becomes the delivered text, whose head line is the error's label. The input logs via the message-accepting overload with a null message and a labeled error; the output reports the severity, tag, head line (the error label) and that the error rendering was serialized into the message.

feature6_tag_derivation.json.description: An automatic tag can be derived from a stack frame's class name for destinations that infer their tag from the caller. The derivation strips the package portion (keeping only the simple class name), removes trailing anonymous-class markers (a run of dollar-sign-plus-digits at the end), and, on platform levels below a fixed threshold, truncates the result to a maximum tag length; at or above that threshold no truncation occurs. The derivation can also be overridden, for example to append the frame's line number to the derived tag. The input supplies a class name, a line number, the platform level, and whether the line-appending override is in effect; the output is the derived tag.

feature7_message_chunking.json.description: A destination that writes to a sink with a per-write length limit splits long messages so that no single write exceeds the limit. The text is first split on newline boundaries, and each resulting line that is longer than the maximum write length is further broken into successive pieces of at most the maximum length. A message short enough to fit in one write is emitted unsplit; a message that exceeds the threshold (overall) is chunked. The input describes a message built from runs of repeated characters separated by newlines; the output reports each emitted chunk's severity, tag, length, and leading character (identifying which run it came from).

cases.expected_output:
priority=3 tag=T len=3000 char=a
priority=3 tag=T len=4000 char=b
priority=3 tag=T len=2000 char=b
priority=3 tag=T len=3000 char=c
